speaker: interviewer
text: --number four for Mr. Kahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Kahn, you were talking earlier about a son who had to leave his father. And I understand you have something to elaborate on that with a poem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That-- this is a-- a poem that was written right after the war was over by an Israeli writer, [? Shimonovich, ?] based on an interview he had with a sick boy, which is indicative of what I told you about the scene that is in my mind about the son leaving his father dying. This is a similar story, which is a very moving story. And I-- I-- I really like to read this here. This is in Hebrew, and I don't have a real translation, but I'll translate it as I read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The story is from a sick boy. And the title of the story is 'He Did Not Keep His Word.' And here is the story. 'How is this possible? My father has fooled me, has lied to me. My father, the righteous, the dear one, the lovely one for who-- who only taught me to tell the truth. Who never would profane, who would consider sacrilegious to utter a word of lie. My father for whom I would have lied, he hated a lie more than he hated death. How is-- was this possible that my father lied to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true the whole time he was pleading with me. And he would say, 'Jump, my son. Jump. Now is the time to jump from the wagon. You jump and maybe God will have pity on you.' And from the dryness of his throat, it was sounding like the sound of somebody that's croaking. To-- my heart simply froze when I listened to him. And again, 'My son, I am already old. And I could not possibly accompany you. You are still young. You just jump and run. Maybe you'll be lucky enough to-- to survive. I will only spoil it for you if I do accompany you. Jump and quickly run into the bushes.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The train is flying. From both sides you see woods, forests, and there's a fog, a heavy fog. The smell is terrible, the smell of death and of human waste. And the screaming in the wagon and the fear is just impossible. And I'm looking at my father's eyes with such a sadness. And I embraced him. And I said to him, 'You, I will not leave.' He quiet down for a while. And then suddenly with such a strength, 'Quick, my son. Quick. The train is going uphill now. And now is the time to jump. Quick. And as soon as you jump, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will jump with you.'

speaker: interviewer
text: He trembled. He pushed me with such a strength. 'Quick, quick, my son. Straight into the bushes. And after you, I will run.' This strength suddenly, where did it come from? I jumped. My heart froze and also boiled. Where's my father? For this, I was not ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father did not keep his word. He stayed back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The train is going downhill now. And there's not a sign from the train. And there's not a sign from my father. Maybe some [? usher ?] someplace in the fields. But that touch, that push from his hand, that he has encouraged me to jump, that touch burns my shoulder and my heart till this day. But more than that fierce touch, astonishment, bewilderment burns me. My father deceived me. My father lying to me. And I will not at all consider myself insane if I'll summon my father to a court up in the heaven, because I am crying day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that possible, my father the righteous, my father the justice, how could he deceive me?'

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the essence of the tragic events that took place almost every day, like the one I saw in the snow, the son leaving his father freezing, and so forth and so on. This-- this poem is a very touchy, descriptive poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- we were talking about the hospital. How long was your stay, and-- and where were you, and-- and what were the events there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in a hospital in 1945, I was separated from my brother. I heard that he got-- he was on a pile of dead bodies in Dachau before the evacuation. And he heard that I was. We both heard from each other that each one is dead. For-- through some miracle, he found out I was transferred from one-- one hospital to the other. And the end of 1945-- we were liberated in April, the 29th. In the end of 1945, he caught up with me. So I was in hospital. He was walking for miles, and he came into the hospital where I was a patient. And he had swollen feet and blisters all over his feet. Some miracle, how-- how he has traced me. And the doctors put a bed right next to me, and he stayed with me. And I was in hospitals from 1945 in a sanatorium for tuberculosis. I had active positive TB. In 1930-- 1947, I had a-- had a relapse. I was very bad. Very bad. I was almost gone, 1947. But again, miraculously, I recouped, rebound, and-- I was in this hospital till 1948. From 1945 till-- from the end of '45, aside from the transition hospitals, I-- and this hospital was Gauting. G-A-U-T-I-N-G. Gauting, which is about 15 kilometers from Munich. It was-- during the war, it was a Wehrmacht hospital, an army hospital for the Germans. And there were several hundred patients, tuberculosis only. And they treated us, German doctors and nuns, very good. And the Joint Distribution Committee and the IRO-- IRO, we called it, International Refugee Organization-- has taken care of us. They supported us. We didn't have anything to our name other than every so often we would get a package from the Joint. We used to call the Joint. And clothing. Every once in a while, all the patients would come to one block, and they would be distributed some clothing. This is all we had. 1948, I was recuperated enough to be sent to a convalescent home. From there, we went to Passau, P-A-S-S-A-U, which is a-- a Bavarian city near the Austrian border. Very picturesque city. Very picturesque. A place-- there was a monastery. They housed us in a monastery which was hundreds of years old. The very historical place, a place where three rivers come together, and one of them is the Danube. The Danube, the Donau, the Inz-- the Ilz, and the Inn-- Inn, big river. Anyway, they-- we were there for about a year, over a year. And we were trained by the ORT since we were already recuperating. I took a radio course. I have a document of having finished the radio course. Sponsored, run by the ORT in this convalescent home. And they set up all kinds of machinery and all kinds of equipment. And they had German instructors, and they trained us. This is to prepare us for our future life. And from Passau, they sent us to Esslingen, another convalescent home. Esslingen, E-S-S-L-I-N-G-E-N, Esslingen. This is a town, a little town not far from Stuttgart. And there again we completed the courses in our training as radio mechanics. And in 1950, I was-- I was applying, naturally, to immigrate to America. I wanted to go to Israel, but my brother did not want to go to Israel. And since we were just the two of us survived from the whole family, I didn't want to separate. He definitely-- I could not convince him to go to Israel. I came with a heavy feeling to America. Never-- he-- nevertheless, I didn't want to separate. So we both came to America. But I was rejected twice because of my health condition. Apparently, I wasn't healed enough. You have to go through a whole strict series of-- of classifications by the CIA, politically, and physically, and so forth. Everything was fine except physically, I was not capable enough. I was rejected twice. On the third pick, I was passed. I came to the United States on May-- the end of May, 1945. All I had in my pocket was $20 and one suit that was rather a heavy suit. And it was unbearably hot and humid. And I thought, I'm going to die from the heat. And I was complaining, and people told me, you'll get used to it. You'll get used to it. I landed here in Philadelphia by coincidence, because a cousin of mine was coming. He was here for over a year before that. I was destined to go to Seattle, Washington. And it's been 46 years I'm here. This morning, I found the address where I was supposed to be reporting in Seattle, Washington. I was astounded by finding the address this morning, as I was looking for the other documentation. I found an address, Seattle, Washington. This is 46 years ago. I came to the United States under the protection-- under the auspices of the Joint Distribution Committee, the Council of American Jewish-- whatever council. There's so many different organizations involved in receiving and settling these people, and the federation, so forth. And I remember I used to go every week to this woman downtown to-- and she gave me all whatever coupons I needed, so forth and so on. Life was rough. It was rough. I did not know any English at all. I knew all other languages but English. But my Yiddish and my Polish and my German, which was fluent, and my Hebrew, was not of much use at the time when I first came to the-- to Philadelphia. But somehow, I picked it up. I was very resilient. I was young. I was lucky. I was fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: People have asked me, how did you survive? By miracles. I don't know how I survived. A miracle happened at one time with a picture. Then I would have been shot with no-- for no reason-- for-- for much lesser transgressions.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time I came home late after the curfew, and people were shot. We saw them. And I somehow was lucky enough to escape it. How did I escape the camps without food going on for so many weeks and months under such adverse conditions such as snows and the-- I remember the-- in May when I was liberated, there was cold. The sun was shining in May, and I-- we-- I kept saying to whoever would listen to me, God didn't promise us enough. We didn't have any clothing. We didn't have any shoes. And it was May the 1st, maybe the 2nd, the 3rd before I collapsed in that line there. And I was freezing cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, it's May already. Maybe God will have mercy on us and at least not punish us. It was a cold May. Snow on the ground. How did we survive it? It's hard to describe it. It's just hard to describe it. It was miracles.

speaker: interviewee
text: But here's the thing. I know most of us, the majority of us, would never talk about it all these years. We didn't want to talk. First of all, maybe there was not too many people willing to listen. Furthermore, it was a stigma. I remember walking with long sleeves in the summertime, not to show my number, my tattoo. I didn't want to be picked out from a crowd. I was conscious of the fact-- we were too busy creating our families, rebuilding our lives. We didn't want to talk of it-- about it. This is a thing of the past. And many a times in company, I would be asked and I would say, I'm sorry. I'd rather not talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden now in the later years, what's happening is history is-- is-- is bearing down on us. We realize that we are being decimated by natural cause, natural-- we realize that if we don't talk now, when are we going to talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: But more than that, I think the real impetus, the real push that made us open up is the fact that there are so many groups now that are denying that the Holocaust ever took place. This boils in my blood when I-- I once answered a-- in here. I answered a column. There was some kind of article about the-- some monologue between the American Jewish Congress and the Polish American people about the Holocaust and about this here. When I read this article which was favor-- in favor of such a meeting, I absolutely exploded. And I wrote a lengthy article. And funny, I didn't think they will-- they'll print it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they printed every word without even editing it. And I says, how can you have any monologues with people who were killing your brothers and sisters? With murderers? I mean, there's nothing to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: But yet somehow things are changing. Times are changing. Priorities are changing. We-- we talk about it now because we realize it's getting late in our lives. And not only this. Ironically, now things coming to fore so bold, those scenes. Instead of forgetting them, instead of-- it's 50 some years since this has happened. We have nightmares constantly, and all over yet the more we talk about it. When we get together now-- years ago, we start a bar mitzvah-- we used to talk about bar mitzvahs and weddings and schooling and sending the kids to school and so forth. Now, we are talking about-- we going to funerals a lot, naturally. But we are talking inadvertently, whenever some gathering comes together, the subject matter is the concentration camps and how we lost our parents and how we lost our fathers, and where did you go from here to there. I mean, these are things to be forgotten that-- that we have talked about it. We haven't talked that much about it. Now, now we are coming out. A lot of factors were instrumental, naturally. The-- the movie, has made a lot of people aware of these things. They-- I have a few letters from people have-- who have written to me after seeing one-- one tape that I made. And it's just-- it's just moving to tears, the reaction of people, total strangers-- not Jewish people-- who have come across-- who have-- I came across accidentally by word of mention, and you got to know that I have some kind of documentations, and I show you them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have-- I have a letter here from somebody that's absolutely moving. So it has an educational value, naturally. A lot of people-- I'll tell you this here. People used to ask me, what is your number and what is this here? And I used to get very nasty about it. I-- I reacted very-- I remember I had a business. And naturally in summertime when I worked, people would come over and say, if you don't mind, I would ask you a personal question. What is this number on your arm? And I was so upset about it. If you haven't known by now what this is, I'm not going to tell you. If you are so ignorant. But I've changed my mind to this fact, because some people may not have been-- done it purposely. They just were too ignorant to have learned history. Not everybody is up on history. One guy asked me, did you have to pay anything for this? No, not a penny. I got it-- I was scot-free. Could you briefly summarize what happened after you came to America? What did you do after your training, and-- and so forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life was rough. I went to work in a factory. I worked there for four years until it got slow, and I was still preferred because I was a good worker. And all this here. I excelled and all this here. And then one time, the foreman tells me, you don't come in tomorrow. You can go back where you came from. Well, I-- this didn't sit too well with me. As much as I needed the job, I hated the place and I hated the man for saying so. He really insulted my intelligence. I also was teaching Hebrew school at that time. As soon as I came, I enrolled at [INAUDIBLE] College. I have a diploma hanging right there, as a matter of fact. Didn't even realize. And I excelled in Hebrew studies. And I was teaching at Hebrew school. And I quit this job in the factory. And then I did a few things which did not really amount to much. I went to farmers' markets, tried to be on sales, and so forth. Until 1959, I realized that in order to do something stable, I have to roll up my sleeves and go to work. And I acquired a-- a luncheonette in Center City. And I was there for 25 years. And until the building was ready for renovation or whatever they-- they cancelled our leases and they got us out. This was in 1983. I have missed this kind of business. I was right in the center of all the activity. Really touching the pulse of life. It was a hard, hard business. But I-- I enjoyed it because it was people. I like people. And after a year and a half not being able to be idle, I went out and I got a job in a-- in a surgical place selling hospital supplies. I worked there for 10 years until last year. I worked there for 10 full years. I became very efficient in-- in different instruments and hospitals supplies and so forth. And a year ago, June a year ago, I was laid off. I was let go. Now, I'm really in between-- I cannot stand being idle. I have-- I still did not find my-- my niche as to what I want to do. But I'm still active, even though I'm 74 years old. But I feel I can still be productive, even not productive in a material way, but I can still be useful in some way or another. I perform services in a retirement home. By the way, for the High Holidays and during the course of the year, I sing in two choirs. I'm not completely idle, but I'm not working. I'm not earning here anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married when? When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married in 1953. And our daughter was born in 1956. She lives in Winnipeg, Canada. It's quite a distance. We don't see her too often at all. We see her once a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's hard on my wife, on myself. We have two grandchildren. They're getting older. They-- they used to come now every summer, once a summer. This summer, they are not coming. The kids went to camp, and they want to have a family vacation together. So let's-- we miss them. And other than my brother that lives in Wilmington here, I don't have too-- I don't have any family at all. I have a cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how would you sum up this experiences that you went through, and what advice would you have for others at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Advice? There's-- how I would sum up? I would sum up it can happen any place. It has no parallel in-- in human history, those things. Nobody would anticipate or expect, how could this have happened? [BACKROUND NOISE] There's just no answers to these questions. How could a civilized country like Germany, highly civilized-- I always admired the Germans, even after the war, ironically. I-- I admire their-- their orderliness, their [? organizationess ?], their-- their love for music and love for beauty, love-- love for art. Yet the same-- very same people gave us such giants in-- in-- in the art-- in the art world, could stoop down to a bestialic-- to such-- to-- to savagely killing, torturing. First-- first they dehumanized us by taking our dignity away, by spitting on us by-- by-- by-- by doing such inhuman things. And later on-- later on they built a-- a very efficient death factory. Very-- with precision. With all kinds of-- of-- of efficiencies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could this have happened in the same time? How-- there's no answer. No people-- no human mind can really absorb the enormity, the-- the immensity, I guess. We see it now in a better light than we saw right after it has happened, because we have to distance ourselves, as I mentioned before, to see how-- how-- how tall the building is and how deep the hole is. We have to distance ourselves on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Advice. It can happen any place. It can-- situations like this brings out the best in people and the worst in people. We have seen the Poles have risked their lives and saved Jews. And you must not forget that this was a risky life. They-- if-- whenever they were caught, they paid with their lives. There were, of course, not too many. And too few and far in between. But there were-- there was a book written not too long ago by a Mr. Silver. He did research on 40 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saviors. People who-- we call them the-- the righteous-- Gentile righteous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he really tried to dig into the-- if there's a common denominator. Why-- they were people from all walks of life. They were people from-- from-- plain, ordinary people. People in-- in-- in-- but usually it was common, everyday people who actually risked their lives to save people. And he asked them why? Why did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's-- it's very interesting reading. Of course, there's an organization now trying to help these people. A lot of them already passed on. And of course, in Jerusalem at the Yad Vashem, at the big Holocaust Memorial, there's a-- a forest, the Forest for the Righteous. And there's trees planted in each one's name. This here is a symbolic monument. It's-- since there's no graves where to go to visit, of course every holiday we say a prayer for the dead. And naturally, I-- I intone and I mention all their names that I-- and I have too many names to-- to mention. But whenever there's a anniversary of yahrzeit, I light these candles. And this is-- this is the grave, and this is their monument. And in it is inscribed the name of my father, the name of my mother, my sister-- the name of my sister, the name of the uncle that I lost on the march, on the death march. And I see his face. He was-- could hardly walk. Next morning, he was nowhere to be found. Probably froze overnight, or got shot. And we live with it. We live-- we live with these memories, and they are not somehow disappearing. They are not getting lost in the-- in the past. Somehow, we will be living with them until the end of our days. And as I said before, as more of our people, the survivors, have come forward now after all these years where before you never heard anything of it. Whatever-- whatever prompted us, I guess, the-- the-- the fact that we subconsciously realize that we are not going to be around for too long. And the fact that so many-- so many revisionists, as they call themselves, denying that this whole thing never took place. And also the fact that came out. There's much more awareness. There's a much more conscious awareness of-- among the non-Jewish people to learn history. There's a lot of-- lot of things, interesting things that are happening nowadays. A lot of German students go back to Poland. And this is part of their-- the seminarians. The Catholic seminary in Warsaw, I understand, has a lot of its students go back to Poland and try to restore Jewish cemeteries. For what, for when. This was a world that was just cut down, exterminated, exterminated, annihilated, forever. It will never be coming back. The whole land of Europe, the whole-- the whole continent is soaked with Jewish blood. And wherever one stands, he can consider it as a cemetery and-- and say Kaddish, and he would be observing the deed of-- of-- of remembering his next of kin. It's something that was and will ever be, and it's just impossible to describe. And to paint the type of life that we have enjoyed in Europe, the-- the vitality, the vibrancy, the-- the genealogy of the Jewish people, the-- the-- Poland, especially, which was the source of Jewish learning and Jewish teaching. All this came to an abrupt stop, an abrupt halt, under such circumstances which are impossible to describe, no matter how eloquent one may be and no matter how much books, how many documentaries, how much film has been made. All of this is just pale in comparison to the actual life that was. These numbers-- this number is relatively--
